title: Debugging healthcheck/health-cmd is hard aka maybe add `--verbose` / `-v` option for health checks
body:
### Feature request description

Background problem was https://github.com/containers/podlet/issues/160 basicaly aka Quadlet generated a broken healthcheck command like this:
```ini
# THIS DOES NOT WORK!
HealthCmd='curl --fail --silent --show-error --max-time 5 http://localhost:9998/ || exit 1'
```

Correct would be:
```ini
HealthCmd=CMD-SHELL curl --fail --silent --show-error --max-time 5 http://localhost:9998/ || exit 1
```

Anyway, this was really hard to debug, because:
* both `podman ps` and `podman healthcheck run` only display "unhealthy" if it fails, o more information (and no information at all if it works, actually 😉)
* the only other way to debug this is `podman exec -it containername /bin/sh` and executing the healthcheck manually, which, in my case, worked. So that's not really an option in any case and may not always work.


### Suggest potential solution

Add an option like `--verbose` to that command, so you can run `podman healthcheck --verbose run containername` and it shows you more information. Like the errors or actual output returned by the healthcheck command or any details about how internally.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

* more docs?
* I mean the actual issue was basically https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/22804 aka https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/25729 which points to https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-systemd.unit.5.html#debugging-unit-files but that, however, does not document `CMD` or `CMD-SHELL` commands at all, as far as I see.
   https://docs.docker.com/reference/compose-file/services/#healthcheck does document the general thing a bit but does not apply directly/only if you come from a compose file like me.
  *  after all, IMHO, I guess this should be done in addition

### Additional context

Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

comments.body:
podman container inspect already includes information about the healtcheck output in the State.Health field, i.e.

```
               "Health": {
                    "Status": "starting",
                    "FailingStreak": 1,
                    "Log": [
                         {
                              "Start": "2025-10-01T12:11:19.624804069+02:00",
                              "End": "2025-10-01T12:11:19.636555946+02:00",
                              "ExitCode": 1,
                              "Output": "/bin/sh: abcd: not found\n"
                         }
                    ]
               },
```

And there is also the Config.Healthcheck field which lists the command
```
               "Healthcheck": {
                    "Test": [
                         "CMD-SHELL",
                         "abcd"
                    ],
                    "Interval": 30000000000,
                    "Timeout": 30000000000,
                    "Retries": 3
               },
```

And for podman run/create there is the `--health-log-destination` option to put the logs to a specific destination.


I am not sure what extra info verbose would add beside the output logging directly to the shell. I guess if that is needed for debugging the logical step is to just add it to the debug log level we already support instead of a new option.

If you run `podman --log-level debug healthcheck run <name>`

It already logs the command it executes:
```
DEBU[0000] executing health check command /bin/sh -c abcd for 202e0841d460dfb698da6f807b0286cf18d45b4ec5733519d51192a7b5c3ef15 
```

Well it's a convenience feature born out of my desire (and I guess it's a common one) for easy debugging. Because well:
* inspect -> oh yeah I could have found that, but that is a very big list and I did not found that there nor thought of searching it
* `--log-level debug` is again a thing one needs to find out so and I was not aware of that, so...

`-v` s just a very common flag so one likely automatically tries that out. And IIRC it works for other commands, does not it?

Otherwise see my OP "other ideas", another idea is to improve the docs.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Hi, I am a student from UT Austin, can I get assigned this issue?

@Luap99 Ok got it. What would your recommendation be for the documentation? As a new contributor, I am not entirely sure what the best approach would be. One idea I had was to add a debugging_tutorial.md to the docs directory to make it slightly more clear as to how to debug errors because trying to find the commands for health checks, while documented, may be a bit difficult for users to know exists, especially if it is nested deep within other directories. Let me know what you think is the best way to approach this!

I cannot give you a solid recommendation, we tend to mainly use the normal command man pages, so that would be like podman-healtcheck or podman-healthcheck-run, you could also add some pointer to the --health options in podman run/create.
I am however the wrong person to know where an average user is going to look for these infos so if others like you think some kind of "debugging" file is better sure I would not mind that either. The thing to note is that the command docs are shipped as proper man page on systems so actually locally available while tutorials only show up on the online docs site I think.
